item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Well, Bob, we lost two MTFs. Now what?"

"Jim, how's about we let the Feds go in and get slaughtered to death and we can tiptoe in after?"

"Righto, Bob. I'll make the call."

+1 for Operation Tipsy Eagle

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think the idea is that rather than undertake an operation that would require a shitload of resources and ensuing coverup theyre electing to let the atf deal w the bulk of the issue (people w guns). they can do spot cleanup where anomalous activity actually takes place in the course of the totally normal "atf dealing with a dangerous cult" operation that doesnt require complete secrecy from the population

item.title: Re: This is really well done
(no title)
item.content:encoded: My best friend is an ATF agent. She frowned when I showed her that line. Automatic +1 from me for causing that reaction (and also because it was a very well written piece that kept me immersed from the beginning to the end. I really love the idea of anomalous Amish using this book to communicate with each other through changing text)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the tone of the documents and I think you do a great job of nailing down that odd religious tone without it sounding similar to one that already exists. My only problems are A. It seems weird that after losing a MTF the Foundation would call in normal atf swat teams not trained to deal with whatever anomalies might be present in the area.

Secondly it feels kind of unfinished. The document seems to imply the atf raid is imminent and not having it resolved feels odd.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While at first I was a little miffed at him calling the SCPF by their full name (in place of a more abstract ephitet) the bathos in the delivery of the final line nudged me over into an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bit of which I'm proudest in this article was coming up with the name "Tipsy Eagle" for the "call the ATF" protocol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, it's up! Good to see, +1.

Fun fact: The Pennsylvania Dutch actually aren't from the Netherlands. The word "Dutch" does not refer to the Dutch people (Nederlanders) or their descendants, but to the German settlers, known as Deutsch (in standard German).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I googled "Pennsylvania Dutch last names" and chose one that I liked the sound of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check before questioning things like that; a quick google search turns up that Glattfelder is an actual (fairly widespread) surname. They have a coat of arms.

item.title: This is really well done
item.content:encoded: Really good. I enjoyed the struck out text and updates, those were very well executed. It was brief in a very enjoyable way, fully realized but quick enough where it never lagged. Great jorb

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Quite enjoyable. Interesting concept executed well.

My only nitpick is the name "GLATTFELDER." The name just sounds so cartoony, I really thought this was going to be one of those humorous alternate universe things like the baseball game or something. It sounds like something out of Rick and Morty or Space Quest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! As somebody who has lived both in the tristate area and New England, there is so much history to explore here. This basically came from the idea of that sort of insular, colonial small town taken to the extreme, along with a witch hunt where it’s the witches doing the hunting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. Coming from the northeast, there’s always that undercurrent of slightly creepy colonial and post colonial culture and history. This does a nice job at tapping into that ethos, and the way the town seemed to fade in and out of our own world was a nice touch.
